results.query: Mem0 architecture for LLM agent memory

verdicts.claim: Mem0 processes conversational message pairs through two phases—memory extraction and consolidation—and uses an asynchronous summary generation module with a recency window hyperparameter to maintain context-aware, up-to-date memories
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes memory extraction, consolidation, and asynchronous summary generation with recency window.

verdicts.claim: Mem0g extends the base architecture with a directed labeled graph representation where entities are nodes and relationships are edges, enabling richer multi-hop relational recall and scoring ~2% higher than base Mem0 on LOCOMO
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states graph memory achieves ~2% higher score than base Mem0 on LOCOMO

verdicts.claim: Mem0 attains 91% lower p95 latency and saves more than 90% in token cost compared to full-context approaches, making it practical for production deployment
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states both metrics in abstract and introduction.

verdicts.claim: CraniMem, a neurocognitively inspired alternative, couples goal-conditioned gating and utility tagging with a bounded episodic buffer and a structured long-term knowledge graph, outperforming both Vanilla RAG and Mem0 under clean and noisy (distractor-injected) conditions
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes CraniMem's architecture and performance claims.

verdicts.claim: CraniMem's scheduled consolidation loop replays high-utility memory traces and prunes low-utility items via importance weighting and temporal decay, demonstrating that selective forgetting outperforms both unlimited retention and aggressive compression
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes CraniMem's consolidation loop and selective forgetting superiority.

verdicts.claim: A unified taxonomy of LLM agent externalization frames memory as state externalization across time, complemented by skills (procedural expertise) and protocols (interaction structure), all coordinated by a harness engineering layer
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states the unified taxonomy with all four components

verdicts.claim: Current long-term memory benchmarks largely test shallow fact retrieval; the Memora benchmark and FAMA metric expose deeper failures by spanning weeks-to-months of conversation and penalizing reliance on obsolete or invalidated memories—under which memory agents show only marginal improvements
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes Memora benchmark, FAMA metric, weeks-to-months conversations, penalizing obsolete memories, and marginal improvements.

verdicts.claim: A hardware-level approach persists per-agent KV caches to disk in 4-bit quantized format, reducing time-to-first-token by up to 136× and fitting 4× more agent contexts into fixed device memory than FP16, offering a complementary low-level memory strategy for edge deployments
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes all claim components with matching metrics.

verdicts.claim: PagedAttention divides the KV cache into fixed-size blocks stored in non-contiguous paged memory, directly inspired by OS virtual memory management, enabling near-zero memory waste
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states PagedAttention divides KV cache into blocks, inspired by OS virtual memory paging, achieving near-zero waste.

verdicts.claim: Existing LLM serving systems waste significant memory: profiling shows only 20.4%–38.2% of KV cache memory is used for actual token states in prior systems
verdicts.reason: Figure 2 shows token states at 20.4%-38.2% across systems

verdicts.claim: vLLM improves throughput by 2–4× over FasterTransformer and Orca baselines at the same latency level, evaluated on GPT, OPT, and LLaMA model families
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states vLLM improves throughput 2-4× over FasterTransformer and Orca

verdicts.claim: In head-to-head benchmarking on 4× NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs, vLLM achieves up to 24× higher throughput than HuggingFace TGI under high-concurrency workloads via PagedAttention
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states vLLM achieves 24x higher throughput via PagedAttention

verdicts.claim: vLLM excels in high-throughput batch scenarios while HuggingFace TGI demonstrates lower tail latencies for interactive single-user scenarios, suggesting workload-dependent framework selection
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states vLLM excels in high-throughput batch scenarios, TGI has lower tail latencies for interactive single-user scenarios, and recommends workload-dependent framework selection.

verdicts.claim: The vLLM engine combines block-level memory management with preemptive request scheduling and supports distributed serving via tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes PagedAttention block-level memory management, request scheduling, and tensor parallelism support.

verdicts.claim: Core implementation components include PagedAttention memory management, continuous batching, and custom CUDA kernels for high-speed processing
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly lists all three components mentioned in claim

verdicts.claim: A plug-in ecosystem (e.g., vLLM Hook) extends vLLM with passive probing and active intervention of internal model states such as attention heads and activations, enabling use cases like prompt injection detection and activation steering
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes vLLM Hook enabling passive probing and active intervention of attention heads, activations, with prompt injection detection and activation steering use cases.

results.query: RAG vs long-context windows: when does each approach win in 2025-2026?

verdicts.claim: Order-preserving RAG (OP-RAG), which places retrieved chunks in their original document order rather than by relevance rank, achieves 44.43 F1 with Llama3.1-70B using only 16K tokens, versus 34.32 F1 for the same model consuming its full 128K context window — a substantial quality gain at a fraction of the token cost
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states exact F1 scores and token counts claimed.

verdicts.claim: Answer quality in RAG follows an inverted-U curve as the number of retrieved chunks grows: performance rises initially, peaks at a 'sweet point,' then declines as noise and context overload accumulate
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly describes inverted-U curve pattern with sweet points

verdicts.claim: Across 20 LLMs tested at context lengths from 2K to 128K tokens, the majority first improve and then degrade in RAG accuracy as context length increases, meaning longer retrieval is not a free lunch
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states majority of models first increase then decrease RAG performance as context length increases

verdicts.claim: Only a small number of state-of-the-art models — including GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, and Gemini 1.5 — can maintain consistent RAG accuracy above 64K tokens; most open-source and older commercial models cannot
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states only handful of state-of-the-art LLMs maintain consistent accuracy above 64K tokens

verdicts.claim: Models fail at long RAG contexts in qualitatively distinct ways: some produce wrong answers while others refuse to respond entirely — for example, Claude 3 Sonnet frequently refused due to perceived copyright concerns at longer context lengths
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly states models fail in distinct ways: some provide incorrect answers, others refuse due to copyright concerns.

verdicts.claim: A comparative evaluation framework revisiting long-context vs. RAG trade-offs confirms that neither approach dominates universally, and that task type and document structure are key moderating variables
verdicts.reason: could not fetch any source body

verdicts.claim: RAG's token efficiency advantage is practically significant for cost and latency: OP-RAG can deliver superior answer quality while consuming orders-of-magnitude fewer tokens than feeding an entire long document to a frontier model
verdicts.reason: Source explicitly demonstrates OP-RAG achieves superior quality with fewer tokens
